plan: Step 1: Create `src/ui/visibilityManager.ts` — a small module that listens to `document.visibilitychange` and exposes `onVisible(cb)` / `onHidden(cb)` subscription hooks plus a `dispose()` method. This centralizes visibility tracking so the render loop, aggregator, and probe sampler can all react without each wiring their own listener. Export a factory function `createVisibilityManager()`.

Step 2: Create `src/ui/motionManager.ts` — a small module that listens to `matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)')` and exposes `isReduced(): boolean` and `onChange(cb)` subscription plus `dispose()`. This extracts the existing logic from `initRenderer.ts` into a reusable, testable unit.

Step 3: Refactor `src/render/initRenderer.ts` — remove the inline `matchMedia` and `visibilitychange` listeners. Instead, accept `visibilityManager` and `motionManager` as optional dependencies (or import them). Use `motionManager.isReduced()` to gate mesh rotation. Use `visibilityManager.onHidden/onVisible` to pause/resume the rAF loop. Update the `dispose()` to unsubscribe from both managers instead of removing raw listeners. Keep the `RendererHandle` interface unchanged.

Step 4: Add `start()` / `stop()` calls to `ProbeSampler` and `MetricsAggregator` driven by visibility state — in `src/main.ts`, subscribe to `visibilityManager.onHidden()` to call `probeSampler.stop()` and `aggregator.stop()`, and `visibilityManager.onVisible()` to call `probeSampler.start()` and `aggregator.start()`. Both classes already have idempotent `start()`/`stop()` (guarded by `timerId !== null`), so no duplicate timers will be created.

Step 5: Wire reduced-motion into the render loop's animation intensity — currently `initRenderer` skips rotation entirely when reduced-motion is active. This already satisfies 'lowers animation intensity'. Verify this is sufficient, or optionally reduce (not eliminate) rotation speed (e.g., `0.001` instead of `0.005`). Keep render calls active so the scene still displays, just without or with minimal motion.

Step 6: Write unit tests in `tests/unit/ui/visibilityManager.test.ts` — test: (a) `onHidden` callback fires when `document.hidden` becomes true, (b) `onVisible` callback fires when tab returns, (c) `dispose()` removes the `visibilitychange` listener, (d) no duplicate callbacks after unsubscribe.

Step 7: Write unit tests in `tests/unit/ui/motionManager.test.ts` — test: (a) `isReduced()` returns initial `matchMedia` state, (b) `onChange` callback fires on media query change, (c) `dispose()` removes the listener.

Step 8: Write unit tests in `tests/unit/hiddenTabThrottling.test.ts` — test the integration wiring: (a) when visibility changes to hidden, `probeSampler.stop()` and `aggregator.stop()` are called, (b) when visibility returns, `.start()` is called on both, (c) `start()` is idempotent (calling it twice doesn't create duplicate timers — already tested in aggregator tests but verify for the wiring), (d) returning to tab resumes cleanly (aggregator produces a snapshot after resume).

Step 9: Update existing `tests/unit/initRenderer.test.ts` — adjust tests to account for the refactored reduced-motion and visibility handling (now using managers instead of raw listeners). Ensure dispose still cleans up properly.

Step 10: Write Playwright e2e smoke test in `tests/e2e/reducedMotion.spec.ts` — (a) launch with `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce` emulation, verify canvas renders but animation is minimal, (b) test tab-hide/show cycle using `page.evaluate(() => document.dispatchEvent(new Event('visibilitychange')))` or Playwright's built-in visibility control, verify no console errors on resume.

Step 11: Create `docs/adr/015-reduced-motion-hidden-tab-throttling.md` — document the decision to centralize visibility and motion management, the throttling strategy (full stop of timers vs. reduced cadence), and the choice to pause metrics collection entirely when hidden rather than just slow it down.
Step 12: Run the full quality gate: `npm run lint && npm run typecheck && npm run test && npm run test:e2e` — fix any issues until all pass.
testing_recommendations: Unit test `visibilityManager`: verify onHidden/onVisible callbacks fire correctly, dispose removes listener, no duplicate callbacks after unsubscribe
Unit test `motionManager`: verify isReduced() reflects matchMedia state, onChange fires on change events, dispose cleans up
Unit test hidden-tab throttling wiring: verify probeSampler.stop() and aggregator.stop() called on hide, .start() called on show, no duplicate timers on rapid hide/show cycles
Unit test initRenderer: update existing tests for refactored motion/visibility handling, verify dispose still cleans up all subscriptions
Unit test idempotency: call start() multiple times without intervening stop() on both ProbeSampler and MetricsAggregator, assert only one timer exists
E2E test reduced-motion: launch Playwright with prefers-reduced-motion:reduce emulation, verify canvas renders a static or near-static scene
E2E test tab visibility cycle: simulate hide/show, verify no console errors and rendering resumes on return
Run full quality gate: lint, typecheck, vitest, playwright all green
risks: Refactoring initRenderer to use managers instead of inline listeners could break existing tests — mitigate by updating tests in the same step
Stopping ProbeSampler on hidden tab means probe history has gaps — acceptable because probes during hidden state are wasted anyway, and the sampler window retains prior samples

Rapid tab switching (hide/show/hide in quick succession) could cause race conditions with async probe fetches in flight — mitigate by verifying start/stop idempotency and that in-flight fetches complete harmlessly
Playwright visibility emulation may behave differently across browsers — use Chromium-only for the smoke test or feature-detect in the test

The ResourceTimingCollector uses PerformanceObserver (event-driven, not timer-based) so it doesn't need throttling, but entries arriving during hidden state are still collected — this is acceptable and low-cost
